edges.description: GFAP aggregation injures astrocytes, activating glial stress responses that secondarily impair neurons and oligodendrocytes.
edges.source: GFAP Aggregation and Rosenthal Fiber Formation

edges.description: GFAP aggregation and Rosenthal fiber pathology are coupled to oxidative stress responses in white matter.
edges.source: GFAP Aggregation and Rosenthal Fiber Formation
edges.target: Oxidative Stress and White Matter Pathology

edges.description: Toxic mutant GFAP in glia disrupts glial glutamate transport, creating a downstream excitotoxic route to neuronal injury.
Astrocyte dysfunction disrupts white matter support, contributing to the frontal-predominant leukoencephalopathy of Type I disease.
Severe early astrocyte and white matter pathology contributes to progressive head enlargement in Type I disease.
Astrocyte-driven white matter and corticospinal pathway dysfunction contributes to progressive spasticity.
Brainstem and bulbar network involvement from astrocyte pathology contributes to dysphagia.
Brainstem and bulbar motor pathway involvement from astrocyte pathology contributes to dysarthria.
Astrocyte pathology in posterior fossa circuits contributes to ataxia in juvenile and adult-onset disease.
Type II brainstem involvement provides the mechanistic route from astrocyte pathology to autonomic dysfunction.
Astrocyte pathology affecting brainstem and cerebellar ocular motor circuits contributes to nystagmus.
Severe early astrocyte and white matter pathology contributes to encephalopathy and feeding difficulty, producing failure to thrive in Type I disease.
Oxidative stress in white matter regions contributes to the leukodystrophy phenotype.
intermediate_mechanisms: corpus callosum and frontal white matter stress response
edges.source: Oxidative Stress and White Matter Pathology

edges.description: GFAP mutant white matter stress and gliosis increase seizure susceptibility.
edges.source: Oxidative Stress and White Matter Pathology

edges.description: Impaired glial glutamate handling provides a plausible excitotoxic route to seizures.
Non-cell-autonomous neuronal apoptosis contributes to progressive developmental regression and neurologic decline.
Oxidation, crosslinking, and ubiquitination decrease GFAP solubility and promote Rosenthal fiber aggregation.
edges.target: GFAP Aggregation and Rosenthal Fiber Formation
GFAP Aggregation and Rosenthal Fiber Formation
GFAP Aggregation and Rosenthal Fiber Formation

nodes.description: Antisense oligonucleotides targeting GFAP mRNA have shown efficacy in mouse and rat models, achieving near-complete elimination of GFAP protein with reversal of Rosenthal fibers, white matter deficits, and motor impairment.

evidence.reference_title: Antisense suppression of glial fibrillary acidic protein as a treatment for Alexander disease.
evidence.snippet: Nearly complete and long-lasting elimination of GFAP occurred in brain and spinal cord following single bolus intracerebroventricular injections, with a striking reversal of Rosenthal fibers and downstream markers of microglial and other stress-related responses

evidence.reference_title: Antisense suppression of glial fibrillary acidic protein as a treatment for Alexander disease.
evidence.snippet: GFAP protein was also cleared from cerebrospinal fluid, demonstrating its potential utility as a biomarker in future clinical applications

evidence.reference_title: Antisense therapy in a rat model of Alexander disease reverses GFAP pathology, white matter deficits, and motor impairment.
evidence.snippet: a single treatment with Gfap-targeted ASO provides long-lasting suppression, reverses GFAP pathology, and, depending on age of treatment, prevents or mitigates white matter deficits and motor impairment

nodes.description: Mutant GFAP-expressing astrocytes become dysfunctional, with activation of stress response pathways and white matter pathology. Elevated GFAP levels shift GFAP solubility and increase the stress response. Dysfunctional astrocytes fail to support neurons and oligodendrocytes, leading to secondary demyelination and neuronal loss.

evidence.reference_title: Alexander disease-associated glial fibrillary acidic protein mutations in mice induce Rosenthal fiber formation and a white matter stress response.
evidence.snippet: further elevation of GFAP via crosses to GFAP transgenic animals leads to a shift in GFAP solubility, an increased stress response, and ultimately death

evidence.reference_title: Antisense suppression of glial fibrillary acidic protein as a treatment for Alexander disease.
evidence.snippet: A key feature of pathogenesis is overexpression and accumulation of GFAP, with formation of characteristic cytoplasmic aggregates known as Rosenthal fibers

evidence.reference_title: Protein misfolding and oxidative stress promote glial-mediated neurodegeneration in an Alexander disease model.
evidence.snippet: Both protein aggregation and oxidative stress contribute to activation of a robust autophagic response in glia

nodes.description: Cerebellar ataxia present in juvenile and adult forms of Alexander disease.

evidence.reference_title: Alexander disease (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0001251 | Ataxia | Frequent (79-30%)

evidence.reference_title: Mutations in GFAP, encoding glial fibrillary acidic protein, are associated with Alexander disease.
evidence.snippet: patients with juvenile or adult forms typically experience ataxia, bulbar signs and spasticity

nodes.description: Autonomic nervous system dysfunction including abnormal sweating, cardiovascular instability, and gastrointestinal dysmotility. A defining feature of Type II Alexander disease, reflecting brainstem involvement.

evidence.reference_title: Alexander disease (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0012332 | Abnormal autonomic nervous system physiology | Occasional (29-5%)

evidence.reference_title: GFAP mutations, age at onset, and clinical subtypes in Alexander disease.
evidence.snippet: Type II is characterized by later onset, autonomic dysfunction, ocular movement abnormalities, bulbar symptoms, and atypical MRI features

nodes.description: Impaired speech articulation due to bulbar involvement, common in juvenile and adult-onset forms.

evidence.reference_title: Alexander disease (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0001260 | Dysarthria | Frequent (79-30%)

evidence.reference_title: Mutations in GFAP, encoding glial fibrillary acidic protein, are associated with Alexander disease.
evidence.snippet: patients with juvenile or adult forms typically experience ataxia, bulbar signs and spasticity, and a more slowly progressive course

nodes.description: Difficulty swallowing, particularly prominent in Type II Alexander disease as part of the bulbar symptom complex.

evidence.reference_title: Alexander disease (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0002015 | Dysphagia | Frequent (79-30%)

evidence.reference_title: GFAP mutations, age at onset, and clinical subtypes in Alexander disease.
evidence.snippet: Type II is characterized by later onset, autonomic dysfunction, ocular movement abnormalities, bulbar symptoms, and atypical MRI features

nodes.description: Poor weight gain and growth failure, particularly in infantile Alexander disease. Often accompanies feeding difficulties and progressive encephalopathy.

evidence.reference_title: Alexander disease (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0001508 | Failure to thrive | Very frequent (99-80%)

evidence.reference_title: GFAP mutations, age at onset, and clinical subtypes in Alexander disease.
evidence.snippet: Type I is characterized by early onset, seizures, macrocephaly, motor delay, encephalopathy, failure to thrive, paroxysmal deterioration, and typical MRI features

nodes.description: Frontal-predominant white matter abnormality on MRI, a hallmark diagnostic imaging feature of Type I Alexander disease.

evidence.reference_title: Mutations in GFAP, encoding glial fibrillary acidic protein, are associated with Alexander disease.
evidence.snippet: Infants with Alexander disease develop a leukoencephalopathy with macrocephaly, seizures and psychomotor retardation

evidence.reference_title: GFAP mutations, age at onset, and clinical subtypes in Alexander disease.
evidence.snippet: Type I is characterized by early onset, seizures, macrocephaly, motor delay, encephalopathy, failure to thrive, paroxysmal deterioration, and typical MRI features

nodes.description: Gain-of-function mutations in GFAP lead to accumulation of misfolded GFAP protein that forms Rosenthal fibers within astrocytes. These inclusions contain GFAP complexed with small heat shock proteins alphaB-crystallin and HSP27. The aggregation overwhelms the protein quality control machinery. Rosenthal fibers originate as small osmiophilic masses deposited on bundles of intermediate filaments and continue to form over time.
nodes.id: GFAP Aggregation and Rosenthal Fiber Formation

evidence.reference_title: Mutations in GFAP, encoding glial fibrillary acidic protein, are associated with Alexander disease.
evidence.snippet: The pathological hallmark of all forms of Alexander disease is the presence of Rosenthal fibers, cytoplasmic inclusions in astrocytes that contain the intermediate filament protein GFAP in association with small heat-shock proteins

evidence.reference_title: GFAP and its role in Alexander disease.
evidence.snippet: the accumulation of GFAP and the formation of characteristic aggregates, called Rosenthal fibers, (ii) the sequestration of the protein chaperones alpha B-crystallin and HSP27 into Rosenthal fibers, and (iii) the activation of both Jnk and the stress response

evidence.reference_title: The origin of Rosenthal fibers and their contributions to astrocyte pathology in Alexander disease.
evidence.snippet: RFs appear to originate as small, osmiophilic masses containing both GFAP and alphaB-crystallin deposited on bundles of intermediate filaments

evidence.reference_title: GFAP / Alexander disease (Definitive)
evidence.snippet: GFAP | HGNC:4235 | Alexander disease | MONDO:0008752 | AD | Definitive

evidence.reference_title: Mutations in GFAP, encoding glial fibrillary acidic protein, are associated with Alexander disease.
evidence.snippet: Alexander disease therefore represents the first example of a primary genetic disorder of astrocytes, one of the major cell types in the vertebrate CNS

evidence.reference_title: GFAP mutations, age at onset, and clinical subtypes in Alexander disease.
evidence.snippet: R79 and R239 GFAP mutations were most common (16.6% and 20.3% of all cases, respectively). These common mutations predicted distinct clinical outcomes, with R239 predicting the most aggressive course

evidence.reference_title: Infantile Alexander disease: spectrum of GFAP mutations and genotype-phenotype correlation.
evidence.snippet: Missense, heterozygous, de novo GFAP mutations were found in exons 1 or 4 for 14 of the 15 patients analyzed

nodes.description: Disease-associated GFAP mutations promote pathological post-translational modifications including aberrant disulfide cross-linking (particularly cysteine-generating mutations like R239C) and ubiquitination. These modifications yield high-molecular-weight insoluble GFAP species that contribute to Rosenthal fiber formation and resist proteolytic clearance.

evidence.reference_title: Glial fibrillary acidic protein is pathologically modified in Alexander disease.
evidence.snippet: We found high molecular weight GFAP species in the RFs of AxD brains, indicating abnormal GFAP crosslinking as a prominent pathological feature of this disease

evidence.reference_title: Glial fibrillary acidic protein is pathologically modified in Alexander disease.
evidence.snippet: we found GFAP was ubiquitinated in RFs of AxD patients

nodes.description: Recurrent heterozygous GFAP R239 substitutions, including R239C and R239H, are the most common hotspot group and predict a more aggressive clinical course. R239 variants follow the toxic GFAP path through aggregation, Rosenthal fibers, astrocyte dysfunction, and severe Type I-weighted neurologic phenotypes.

evidence.reference_title: Infantile Alexander disease: spectrum of GFAP mutations and genotype-phenotype correlation.
evidence.snippet: Nine patients carried arginine mutations (four had R79H; four had R239C; and one had R239H)

evidence.reference_title: GFAP mutations, age at onset, and clinical subtypes in Alexander disease.
evidence.snippet: These common mutations predicted distinct clinical outcomes, with R239 predicting the most aggressive course

nodes.description: Recurrent heterozygous GFAP R79 substitutions, especially R79H, are a common Alexander disease hotspot. They enter the canonical toxic gain-of-function path from mutant GFAP through Rosenthal fiber formation, astrocyte dysfunction, white matter injury, and subtype-specific neurologic phenotypes.

evidence.reference_title: Infantile Alexander disease: spectrum of GFAP mutations and genotype-phenotype correlation.
evidence.snippet: Nine patients carried arginine mutations (four had R79H; four had R239C; and one had R239H)

evidence.reference_title: GFAP mutations, age at onset, and clinical subtypes in Alexander disease.
evidence.snippet: R79 and R239 GFAP mutations were most common (16.6% and 20.3% of all cases, respectively)

nodes.description: Cysteine-generating GFAP variants, represented in Alexander disease cohorts by R239C and R88C, provide a variant-specific path into abnormal GFAP oxidation and crosslinking before aggregate and Rosenthal fiber formation.

evidence.reference_title: Infantile Alexander disease: spectrum of GFAP mutations and genotype-phenotype correlation.
evidence.snippet: mutations, of which two affect arginine (2R88C and 1R88S) and two affect nonarginine residues (1L76F and 1N77Y)

evidence.reference_title: Glial fibrillary acidic protein is pathologically modified in Alexander disease.
evidence.snippet: In vitro and cell-based studies demonstrate that cystine-generating mutations promote GFAP crosslinking by cysteine-dependent oxidation

nodes.description: Dysfunctional astrocytes in Alexander disease show impaired glutamate uptake via astrocytic transporters, leading to toxic accumulation of extracellular glutamate. This non-cell-autonomous mechanism causes neuronal apoptosis dependent on glial glutamate transport.

evidence.reference_title: Protein misfolding and oxidative stress promote glial-mediated neurodegeneration in an Alexander disease model.
evidence.snippet: Toxicity of mutant GFAP to glial cells induces a non-cell-autonomous stress response and subsequent apoptosis in neurons, which is dependent on glial glutamate transport

nodes.description: Progressive enlargement of the head, particularly prominent in Type I (infantile) Alexander disease. Often the presenting feature.

evidence.reference_title: Alexander disease (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0000256 | Macrocephaly | Very frequent (99-80%)

evidence.reference_title: Mutations in GFAP, encoding glial fibrillary acidic protein, are associated with Alexander disease.
evidence.snippet: Infants with Alexander disease develop a leukoencephalopathy with macrocephaly, seizures and psychomotor retardation

evidence.reference_title: GFAP mutations, age at onset, and clinical subtypes in Alexander disease.
evidence.snippet: Type I is characterized by early onset, seizures, macrocephaly, motor delay, encephalopathy, failure to thrive, paroxysmal deterioration, and typical MRI features

nodes.description: Involuntary rhythmic eye movements reflecting cerebellar or brainstem involvement, observed particularly in adult-onset Alexander disease as part of ocular movement abnormalities.

evidence.reference_title: Alexander disease (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0000639 | Nystagmus | Frequent (79-30%)

evidence.reference_title: GFAP mutations, age at onset, and clinical subtypes in Alexander disease.
evidence.snippet: Type II is characterized by later onset, autonomic dysfunction, ocular movement abnormalities, bulbar symptoms, and atypical MRI features

nodes.description: GFAP mutations induce an antioxidant/oxidative stress response in white matter, with iron accumulation in corpus callosum. Knock-in mice with GFAP mutations show a distinct pattern of stress response gene induction particularly prominent in white matter regions.
nodes.id: Oxidative Stress and White Matter Pathology

evidence.reference_title: Alexander disease-associated glial fibrillary acidic protein mutations in mice induce Rosenthal fiber formation and a white matter stress response.
evidence.snippet: when crossed with an antioxidant response element reporter line, the mutant mice show a distinct pattern of reporter-gene induction that is especially prominent in the corpus callosum, and histochemical staining reveals accumulation of iron in the same region

nodes.description: Loss of previously acquired developmental milestones, characteristic of infantile Alexander disease, leading to death usually within the first decade.

evidence.reference_title: Alexander disease (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0002376 | Developmental regression | Occasional (29-5%)

evidence.reference_title: Mutations in GFAP, encoding glial fibrillary acidic protein, are associated with Alexander disease.
evidence.snippet: Infants with Alexander disease develop a leukoencephalopathy with macrocephaly, seizures and psychomotor retardation, leading to death usually within the first decade

nodes.description: Seizures are common in Type I Alexander disease, often presenting in the first year of life. GFAP mutant mice also show increased susceptibility to kainate-induced seizures.

evidence.reference_title: Alexander disease (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0001250 | Seizure | Very frequent (99-80%)

evidence.reference_title: Mutations in GFAP, encoding glial fibrillary acidic protein, are associated with Alexander disease.
evidence.snippet: Infants with Alexander disease develop a leukoencephalopathy with macrocephaly, seizures and psychomotor retardation

evidence.reference_title: Alexander disease-associated glial fibrillary acidic protein mutations in mice induce Rosenthal fiber formation and a white matter stress response.
evidence.snippet: The mutant mice have a normal lifespan and show no overt behavioral defects, but are more susceptible to kainate-induced seizures

nodes.description: Progressive spasticity affecting the limbs, present in both Type I and Type II Alexander disease. In juvenile and adult forms, spasticity is a prominent feature alongside bulbar signs.

evidence.reference_title: Alexander disease (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0001257 | Spasticity | Very frequent (99-80%)

evidence.reference_title: Mutations in GFAP, encoding glial fibrillary acidic protein, are associated with Alexander disease.
evidence.snippet: patients with juvenile or adult forms typically experience ataxia, bulbar signs and spasticity, and a more slowly progressive course
